html:
XXIV

ClydeтАЩs testimony proceeded to the point where the family had removed from Quincy, Illinois (a place resorted to on account of some Salvation Army work offered his father and mother), to Kansas City, where from his twelfth to his fifteenth year he had browsed about trying to find something to do while still resenting the combination of school and religious work expected of him.

тАЬWere you up with your classes in the public schools?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir. We had moved too much.тАЭ

тАЬIn what grade were you when you were twelve years old?тАЭ

тАЬWell, I should have been in the seventh but I was only in the sixth. ThatтАЩs why I didnтАЩt like it.тАЭ

тАЬAnd how about the religious work of your parents?тАЭ

тАЬWell, it was all rightтБатАФonly I never did like going out nights on the street corners.тАЭ

And so on, through five-and-ten cent store, soda and newspaper carrier jobs, until at last he was a bellhop at the Green-Davidson, the finest hotel in Kansas City, as he informed them.

тАЬBut now, Clyde,тАЭ proceeded Jephson who, fearful lest Mason on the cross-examination and in connection with ClydeтАЩs credibility as a witness should delve into the matter of the wrecked car and the slain child in Kansas City and so mar the effect of the story he was now about to tell, was determined to be beforehand in this. Decidedly, by questioning him properly he could explain and soften all that, whereas if left to Mason it could be tortured into something exceedingly dark indeed. And so now he continued:

тАЬAnd how long did you work there?тАЭ

тАЬA little over a year.тАЭ

тАЬAnd why did you leave?тАЭ

тАЬWell, it was on account of an accident.тАЭ

тАЬWhat kind of an accident?тАЭ

And here Clyde, previously prepared and drilled as to all this plunged into the details which led up to and included the death of the little girl and his flightтБатАФwhich Mason, true enough, had been intending to bring up. But, now, as he listened to all this, he merely shook his head and grunted ironically, тАЬHeтАЩd better go into all that,тАЭ he commented. And Jephson, sensing the import of what he was doingтБатАФhow most likely he was, as he would have phrased it, тАЬspikingтАЭ one of Mr.┬аMasonтАЩs best guns, continued with:

тАЬHow old were you then, Clyde, did you say?тАЭ

тАЬBetween seventeen and eighteen.тАЭ

тАЬAnd do you mean to tell me,тАЭ he continued, after he had finished with all of the questions he could think of in connection with all this, тАЬthat you didnтАЩt know that you might have gone back there, since you were not the one who took the car, and after explaining it all, been paroled in the custody of your parents?тАЭ

тАЬObject!тАЭ shouted Mason. тАЬThereтАЩs no evidence here to show that he could have returned to Kansas City and been paroled in the custody of his parents.тАЭ

тАЬObjection sustained!тАЭ boomed the judge from his high throne. тАЬThe defense will please confine itself a little more closely to the letter of the testimony.тАЭ

тАЬException,тАЭ noted Belknap, from his seat.

тАЬNo, sir. I didnтАЩt know that,тАЭ replied Clyde, just the same.

тАЬAnyhow was that the reason after you got away that you changed your name to Tenet as you told me?тАЭ continued Jephson.

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬBy the way, just where did you get that name of Tenet, Clyde?тАЭ

тАЬIt was the name of a boy I used to play with in Quincy.тАЭ

тАЬWas he a good boy?тАЭ

тАЬObject!тАЭ called Mason, from his chair. тАЬIncompetent, immaterial, irrelevant.тАЭ

тАЬOh, he might have associated with a good boy in spite of what you would like to have the jury believe, and in that sense it is very relevant,тАЭ sneered Jephson.

тАЬObjection sustained!тАЭ boomed Justice Oberwaltzer.

тАЬBut didnтАЩt it occur to you at the time that he might object or that you might be doing him an injustice in using his name to cover the identity of a fellow who was running away?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sirтБатАФI thought there were lots of Tenets.тАЭ

An indulgent smile might have been expected at this point, but so antagonistic and bitter was the general public toward Clyde that such levity was out of the question in this courtroom.

тАЬNow listen, Clyde,тАЭ continued Jephson, having, as he had just seen, failed to soften the mood of the throng, тАЬyou cared for your mother, did you?тБатАФor didnтАЩt you?тАЭ

Objection and argument finally ending in the question being allowed.

тАЬYes, sir, certainly I cared for her,тАЭ replied ClydeтБатАФbut after a slight hesitancy which was noticeableтБатАФa tightening of the throat and a swelling and sinking of the chest as he exhaled and inhaled.

тАЬMuch?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sirтБатАФmuch.тАЭ He didnтАЩt venture to look at anyone now.

тАЬHadnтАЩt she always done as much as she could for you, in her way?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬWell, then, Clyde, how was it, after all that, and even though that dreadful accident had occurred, you could run away and stay away so long without so much as one word to tell her that you were by no means as guilty as you seemed and that she shouldnтАЩt worry because you were working and trying to be a good boy again?тАЭ

тАЬBut I did write herтБатАФonly I didnтАЩt sign my name.тАЭ

тАЬI see. Anything else?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir. I sent her a little money. Ten dollars once.тАЭ

тАЬBut you didnтАЩt think of going back at all?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir. I was afraid that if I went back they might arrest me.тАЭ

тАЬIn other words,тАЭ and here Jephson emphasized this with great clearness, тАЬyou were a moral and mental coward, as Mr.┬аBelknap, my colleague, said.тАЭ

тАЬI object to this interpretation of this defendantтАЩs testimony for the benefit of the jury!тАЭ interrupted Mason.

тАЬThis defendantтАЩs testimony really needs no interpretation. It is very plain and honest, as anyone can see,тАЭ quickly interjected Jephson.

тАЬObjection sustained!тАЭ called the judge. тАЬProceed. Proceed.тАЭ

тАЬAnd it was because you were a moral and mental coward as I see it, ClydeтБатАФnot that I am condemning you for anything that you cannot help. (After all, you didnтАЩt make yourself, did you?)тАЭ

But this was too much, and the judge here cautioned him to use more discretion in framing his future questions.

тАЬThen you went about in Alton, Peoria, Bloomington, Milwaukee, and ChicagoтБатАФhiding away in small rooms in back streets and working as a dishwasher or soda fountain man, or a driver, and changing your name to Tenet when you really might have gone back to Kansas City and resumed your old place?тАЭ continued Jephson.

тАЬI object! I object!тАЭ yelled Mason. тАЬThere is no evidence here to show that he could have gone there and resumed his old place.тАЭ

тАЬObjection sustained,тАЭ ruled Oberwaltzer, although at the time in JephsonтАЩs pocket was a letter from Francis X. Squires, formerly captain of the bellhops of the Green-Davidson at the time Clyde was there, in which he explained that apart from the one incident in connection with the purloined automobile, he knew nothing derogatory to Clyde; and that always previously, he had found him prompt, honest, willing, alert and well-mannered. Also that at the time the accident occurred, he himself had been satisfied that Clyde could have been little else than one of those led and that if he had returned and properly explained matters he would have been reinstated. It was irrelevant.

Thereafter followed ClydeтАЩs story of how, having fled from the difficulties threatening him in Kansas City and having wandered here and there for two years, he had finally obtained a place in Chicago as a driver and later as a bellboy at the Union League, and also how while still employed at the first of these places he had written his mother and later at her request was about to write his uncle, when, accidentally meeting him at the Union League, he was invited by him to come to Lycurgus. And thereupon, in their natural order, followed all of the details, of how he had gone to work, been promoted and instructed by his cousin and the foreman as to the various rules, and then later how he had met Roberta and still later Miss X. But in between came all the details as to how and why he had courted Roberta Alden, and how and why, having once secured her love he felt and thought himself contentтБатАФbut how the arrival of Miss X, and her overpowering fascination for him, had served completely to change all his notions in regard to Roberta, and although he still admired her, caused him to feel that never again as before could he desire to marry her.

But Jephson, anxious to divert the attention of the jury from the fact that Clyde was so very fickleтБатАФa fact too trying to be so speedily introduced into the caseтБатАФat once interposed with:

тАЬClyde! You really loved Roberta Alden at first, didnтАЩt you?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬWell, then, you must have known, or at least you gathered from her actions, from the first, didnтАЩt you, that she was a perfectly good and innocent and religious girl.тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, thatтАЩs how I felt about her,тАЭ replied Clyde, repeating what he had been told to say.

тАЬWell, then, just roughly now, without going into detail, do you suppose you could explain to yourself and this jury how and why and where and when those changes came about which led to that relationship which we all of usтАЭ (and here he looked boldly and wisely and coldly out over the audience and then afterwards upon the jurors) тАЬdeplore. How was it, if you thought so highly of her at first that you could so soon afterwards descend to this evil relationship? DidnтАЩt you know that all men, and all women also, view it as wrong, and outside of marriage unforgivableтБатАФa statutory crime?тАЭ

The boldness and ironic sting of this was sufficient to cause at first a hush, later a slight nervous tremor on the part of the audience which, Mason as well as Justice Oberwaltzer noting, caused both to frown apprehensively. Why, this brazen young cynic! How dared he, via innuendo and in the guise of serious questioning, intrude such a thought as this, which by implication at least picked at the very foundations of societyтБатАФreligious and moral! At the same time there he was, standing boldly and leoninely, the while Clyde replied:

тАЬYes, sir, I suppose I didтБатАФcertainlyтБатАФbut I didnтАЩt try to seduce her at first or at any time, really. I was in love with her.тАЭ

тАЬYou were in love with her?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬVery much?тАЭ

тАЬVery much.тАЭ

тАЬAnd was she as much in love with you at that time?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, she was.тАЭ

тАЬFrom the very first?тАЭ

тАЬFrom the very first.тАЭ

тАЬShe told you so?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬAt the time she left the NewtonsтБатАФyou have heard all the testimony here in regard to thatтБатАФdid you induce or seek to induce her in any way, by any trick or agreement, to leave there?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir, I didnтАЩt. She wanted to leave there of her own accord. She wanted me to help her find a place.тАЭ

тАЬShe wanted you to help her find a place?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬAnd just why?тАЭ

тАЬBecause she didnтАЩt know the city very well and she thought maybe I could tell her where there was a nice room she could getтБатАФone that she could afford.тАЭ

тАЬAnd did you tell her about the room she took at the GilpinsтАЩ?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir, I didnтАЩt. I never told her about any room. She found it herself.тАЭ (This was the exact answer he had memorized.)

тАЬBut why didnтАЩt you help her?тАЭ

тАЬBecause I was busy, days and most evenings. And besides I thought she knew better what she wanted than I didтБатАФthe kind of people and all.тАЭ

тАЬDid you personally ever see the Gilpin place before she went there?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir.тАЭ

тАЬEver have any discussion with her before she moved there as to the kind of a room she was to takeтБатАФits position as regards to entrance, exit, privacy, or anything of that sort?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir, I never did.тАЭ

тАЬNever insisted, for instance, that she take a certain type of room which you could slip in and out of at night or by day without being seen?тАЭ

тАЬI never did. Besides, no one could very well slip in or out of that house without being seen.тАЭ

тАЬAnd why not?тАЭ

тАЬBecause the door to her room was right next to the door to the general front entrance where everybody went in and out and anybody that was around could see.тАЭ That was another answer he had memorized.

тАЬBut you slipped in and out, didnтАЩt you?тАЭ

тАЬWell, yes, sirтБатАФthat is, we both decided from the first that the less we were seen together anywhere, the better.тАЭ

тАЬOn account of that factory rule?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sirтБатАФon account of that factory rule.тАЭ

And then the story of his various difficulties with Roberta, due to Miss X coming into his life.

тАЬNow, Clyde, we will have to go into the matter of this Miss X a little. Because of an agreement between the defense and the prosecution which you gentlemen of the jury fully understand, we can only touch on this incidentally, since it all concerns an entirely innocent person whose real name can be of no service here anyhow. But some of the facts must be touched upon, although we will deal with them as light as possible, as much for the sake of the innocent living as the worthy dead. And I am sure Miss Alden would have it so if she were alive. But now in regard to Miss X,тАЭ he continued, turning to Clyde, тАЬit is already agreed by both sides that you met her in Lycurgus some time in November or December of last year. That is correct, is it not?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, that is correct,тАЭ replied Clyde, sadly.

тАЬAnd that at once you fell very much in love with her?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir. ThatтАЩs true.тАЭ

тАЬShe was rich?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬBeautiful?тАЭ

тАЬI believe it is admitted by all that she is,тАЭ he said to the court in general without requiring or anticipating a reply from Clyde, yet the latter, so thoroughly drilled had he been, now replied: тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬHad you twoтБатАФyourself and Miss Alden, I meanтБатАФat that time when you first met Miss X already established that illicit relationship referred to?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬWell, now, in view of all thatтБатАФbut no, one moment, there is something else I want to ask you firstтБатАФnow, let me seeтБатАФat the time that you first met this Miss X you were still in love with Roberta Alden, were youтБатАФor were you not?тАЭ

тАЬI was still in love with herтБатАФyes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬYou had not, up to that time at least, in any way become weary of her? Or had you?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir. I had not.тАЭ

тАЬHer love and her companionship were just as precious and delightful to you as ever?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, they were.тАЭ

And as Clyde said that, he was thinking back and it seemed to him that what he had just said was really true. It was true that just before meeting Sondra he was actually at the zenith of content and delight with Roberta.

тАЬAnd what, if any, were your plans for your future with Miss AldenтБатАФbefore you met this Miss X? You must have thought at times of that, didnтАЩt you?тАЭ

тАЬWell, not exactly,тАЭ (and as he said this he licked his lips in sheer nervousness). тАЬYou see, I never had any real plan to do anythingтБатАФthat is, to do anything that wasnтАЩt quite right with her. And neither did she, of course. We just drifted kinda, from the first. It was being alone there so much, maybe. She hadnтАЩt taken up with anybody yet and I hadnтАЩt either. And then there was that rule that kept me from taking her about anywhere, and once we were together, of course we just went on without thinking very much about it, I supposeтБатАФeither of us.тАЭ

тАЬYou just drifted because nothing had happened as yet and you didnтАЩt suppose anything would. Is that the way?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir. I mean, yes, sir. ThatтАЩs the way it was.тАЭ Clyde was very eager to get those much-rehearsed and very important answers, just right.

тАЬBut you must have thought of somethingтБатАФone or both of you. You were twenty-one and she was twenty-three.тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir. I suppose we didтБатАФI suppose I did think of something now and then.тАЭ

тАЬAnd what was it that you thought? Can you recollect?тАЭ

тАЬWell, yes, sir. I suppose I can. That is, I know that I did think at times that if things went all right and I made a little more money and she got a place somewhere else, that I would begin taking her out openly, and then afterwards maybe, if she and I kept on caring for each other as we did then, marry her, maybe.тАЭ

тАЬYou actually thought of marrying her then, did you?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir. I know I did in the way that IтАЩve said, of course.тАЭ

тАЬBut that was before you met this Miss X?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, that was before that.тАЭ

(тАЬBeautifully done!тАЭ observed Mason, sarcastically, under his breath to State Senator Redmond. тАЬExcellent stage play,тАЭ replied Redmond in a stage whisper.)

тАЬBut did you ever tell her in so many words?тАЭ continued Jephson.

тАЬWell, no, sir. I donтАЩt recall that I didтБатАФnot just in so many words.тАЭ

тАЬYou either told her or you didnтАЩt tell her. Now, which was it?тАЭ

тАЬWell, neither, quite. I used to tell her that I loved her and that I never wanted her to leave me and that I hoped she never would.тАЭ

тАЬBut not that you wanted to marry her?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir. Not that I wanted to marry her.тАЭ

тАЬWell, well, all right!тБатАФand sheтБатАФwhat did she say?тАЭ

тАЬThat she never would leave me,тАЭ replied Clyde, heavily and fearsomely, thinking, as he did so, of RobertaтАЩs last cries and her eyes bent on him. And he took from his pocket a handkerchief and began to wipe his moist, cold face and hands.

(тАЬWell staged!тАЭ murmured Mason, softly and cynically. тАЬPretty shrewdтБатАФpretty shrewd!тАЭ commented Redmond, lightly.)

тАЬBut, tell me,тАЭ went on Jephson, softly and coldly, тАЬfeeling as you did about Miss Alden, how was it that upon meeting this Miss X, you could change so quickly? Are you so fickle that you donтАЩt know your own mind from day to day?тАЭ

тАЬWell, I didnтАЩt think so up to that timeтБатАФno, sir!тАЭ

тАЬHad you ever had a strong and binding love affair at any time in your life before you met Miss Alden?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir.тАЭ

тАЬBut did you consider this one with Miss Alden strong and bindingтБатАФa true love affairтБатАФup to the time you met this Miss X?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, I did.тАЭ

тАЬAnd afterwardsтБатАФthen what?тАЭ

тАЬWellтБатАФafterwardsтБатАФit wasnтАЩt quite like that any more.тАЭ

тАЬYou mean to say that on sight of Miss X, after encountering her once or twice, you ceased to care for Miss Alden entirely?тАЭ

тАЬWell, no, sir. It wasnтАЩt quite like that,тАЭ volunteered Clyde, swiftly and earnestly. тАЬI did continue to care for her someтБатАФquite a lot, really. But before I knew it I had completely lost my head overтБатАФover MissтБатАФMissтБатАФтАЭ

тАЬYes, this Miss X. We know. You fell madly and unreasonably in love with her. Was that the way of it?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬAnd then?тАЭ

тАЬWellтБатАФand thenтБатАФI just couldnтАЩt care for Miss Alden so much any more.тАЭ A thin film of moisture covered ClydeтАЩs forehead and cheeks as he spoke.

тАЬI see! I see!тАЭ went on Jephson, oratorically and loudly, having the jury and audience in mind. тАЬA case of the Arabian Nights, of the enscorcelled and the enscorcellor.тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt think I know what you mean,тАЭ said Clyde.

тАЬA case of being bewitched, my poor boyтБатАФby beauty, love, wealth, by things that we sometimes think we want very, very much, and cannot ever haveтБатАФthat is what I mean, and that is what much of the love in the world amounts to.тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir,тАЭ replied Clyde, quite innocently, concluding rightly that this was mere show of rhetoric on JephsonтАЩs part.

тАЬBut what I want to know isтБатАФhow was it that loving Miss Alden as much as you say you didтБатАФand having reached that relationship which should have been sanctified by marriageтБатАФhow was it that you could have felt so little bound or obligated to her as to entertain the idea of casting her over for this Miss X? Now just how was that? I would like to know, and so would this jury, I am sure. Where was your sense of gratitude? Your sense of moral obligation? Do you mean to say that you have none? We want to know.тАЭ

This was really cross-examinationтБатАФan attack on his own witness. Yet Jephson was within his rights and Mason did not interfere.

тАЬWellтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ and here Clyde hesitated and stumbled, quite as if he had not been instructed as to all this beforehand, and seemed to and did truly finger about in his own mind or reason for some thought that would help him to explain all this. For although it was true that he had memorized the answer, now that he was confronted by the actual question here in court, as well as the old problem that had so confused and troubled him in Lycurgus, he could scarcely think clearly of all he had been told to say, but instead twisted and turned, and finally came out with:

тАЬThe fact is, I didnтАЩt think about those things at all very much. I couldnтАЩt after I saw her. I tried to at times, but I couldnтАЩt. I only wanted her and I didnтАЩt want Miss Alden any more. I knew I wasnтАЩt doing rightтБатАФexactlyтБатАФand I felt sorry for RobertaтБатАФbut just the same I didnтАЩt seem able to do anything much about it. I could only think of Miss X and I couldnтАЩt think of Roberta as I had before no matter how hard I tried.тАЭ

тАЬDo you mean to say that you didnтАЩt suffer in your own conscience on account of this?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, I suffered,тАЭ replied Clyde. тАЬI knew I wasnтАЩt doing right, and it made me worry a lot about her and myself, but just the same I didnтАЩt seem to be able to do any better.тАЭ (He was repeating words that Jephson had written out for him, although at the time he first read them he felt them to be fairly true. He had suffered some.)

тАЬAnd then?тАЭ

тАЬWell, then she began to complain because I didnтАЩt go round to see her as much as before.тАЭ

тАЬIn other words, you began to neglect her.тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir, someтБатАФbut not entirelyтБатАФno, sir.тАЭ

тАЬWell, when you found you were so infatuated with this Miss X, what did you do? Did you go and tell Miss Alden that you were no longer in love with her but in love with someone else?тАЭ

тАЬNo, I didnтАЩt. Not then.тАЭ

тАЬWhy not then? Did you think it fair and honorable to be telling two girls at once that you cared for them?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir, but it wasnтАЩt quite like that either. You see at that time I was just getting acquainted with Miss X, and I wasnтАЩt telling her anything. She wouldnтАЩt let me. But I knew then, just the same, that I couldnтАЩt care for Miss Alden any more.тАЭ

тАЬBut what about the claim Miss Alden had on you? DidnтАЩt you feel that that was enough or should be, to prevent you from running after another girl?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬWell, why did you then?тАЭ

тАЬI couldnтАЩt resist her.тАЭ

тАЬMiss X, you mean?тАЭ

тАЬYes, sir.тАЭ

тАЬAnd so you continued to run after her until you had made her care for you?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir, that wasnтАЩt the way at all.тАЭ

тАЬWell then, what was the way?тАЭ

тАЬI just met her here and there and got crazy about her.тАЭ

тАЬI see. But still you didnтАЩt go and tell Miss Alden that you couldnтАЩt care for her any longer?тАЭ

тАЬNo, sir. Not then.тАЭ

тАЬAnd why not?тАЭ

тАЬBecause I thought it would hurt her, and I didnтАЩt want to do that.тАЭ

тАЬOh, I see. You didnтАЩt have the moral or mental courage to do it then?тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt know about the moral or mental courage,тАЭ replied Clyde, a little hurt and irritated by this description of himself, тАЬbut I felt sorry for her just the same. She used to cry and I didnтАЩt have the heart to tell her anything.тАЭ

тАЬI see. Well, let it stand that way, if you want to. But now answer me one other thing. That relationship between you twoтБатАФwhat about thatтБатАФafter you knew that you didnтАЩt care for her any more. Did that continue?тАЭ

тАЬWell, no, sir, not so very long, anyhow,тАЭ replied Clyde, most nervously and shamefacedly. He was thinking of all the people before him nowтБатАФof his motherтБатАФSondraтБатАФof all the people throughout the entire United StatesтБатАФwho would read and so know. And on first being shown these questions weeks and weeks before he had wanted to know of Jephson what the use of all that was. And Jephson had replied: тАЬEducational effect. The quicker and harder we can shock тАЩem with some of the real facts of life around here, the easier it is going to be for you to get a little more sane consideration of what your problem was. But donтАЩt worry your head over that now. When the time comes, just answer тАЩem and leave the rest to us. We know what weтАЩre doing.тАЭ And so now Clyde added:
